primary_diagnosis.name: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (critical limb ischaemia) of the right lower limb secondary to peripheral arterial disease

comorbidities.name: Elevated blood pressure reading (uncontrolled/undiagnosed hypertension possible)

pathophysiology_summary: Long-standing atherosclerosis of the lower-limb arteries, driven by heavy tobacco use, has progressively narrowed and occluded the arterial supply to the right leg. Early on this produced intermittent claudication (reproducible calf pain on exertion relieved by rest) as flow met resting demand but not exertional demand. Over the last year the disease has advanced to the point where resting perfusion is inadequate: ischaemic rest pain now occurs, characteristically worst when lying flat (loss of the gravitational assist to distal flow) and partially relieved by hanging the leg dependent. The examination correlates — diminished popliteal and absent dorsalis pedis/posterior tibial pulses, cool pale shiny skin, hair loss, delayed capillary refill, and a positive Buerger's test (elevation pallor with dependent rubor) — reflect critical macrovascular flow limitation and an anticipated ankle-brachial index well below 0.4. This is chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (Fontaine III / Rutherford category 4): the limb is viable but threatened, and without urgent revascularisation and absolute smoking cessation it will progress to tissue loss, gangrene and amputation. The key differential to exclude is acute limb ischaemia (sudden embolic/thrombotic occlusion) which threatens the limb within hours and is a surgical emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Ischaemic rest pain in the toes, worst lying flat and relieved by dependency, is close to pathognomonic of advanced peripheral arterial disease.

differential.diagnosis: Acute limb ischaemia (embolic or in-situ thrombotic arterial occlusion)
differential.evidence_basis: A sudden, cold, numb, paralysed, pulseless limb is a surgical emergency threatening the limb within hours; must be actively excluded even when a chronic story dominates.
Burning nocturnal foot pain can be neuropathic; but neuropathy does not cause absent pulses, dependency relief or positional colour change.
A painful leg raises DVT, but DVT gives a swollen, warm, red limb — not a cool, pale, pulseless one.

differential_progression.scoring_note: From the opening story the reasoner should already have both chronic limb-threatening ischaemia AND acute limb ischaemia in mind and be moving to screen for the acute emergency.

differential.evidence_basis: Year-long claudication now progressed to dependency-relieved rest pain with cool pale hairless skin — the classic evolution of advanced peripheral arterial disease.
Still on the list as a contributor, but the vascular signs point to macrovascular flow limitation rather than isolated nerve damage.
The limb is cool, pale and pulseless rather than swollen, warm and erythematous — the venous picture is absent.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Neuropathy and DVT recede; the arterial diagnosis firms up. The acute-vs-chronic ischaemia question is the live safety issue.

differential.diagnosis: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (critical limb ischaemia), limb viable but threatened
differential.evidence_basis: Toes remain sensate and movable and worsening was over days — chronic critical ischaemia rather than acute occlusion; still a limb-threatening emergency needing urgent vascular assessment.
Screened negative here (no sudden onset, no numbness/paralysis), but acute-on-chronic deterioration can occur at any time and mandates immediate 000 if it does.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Correctly separating chronic critical ischaemia (urgent, T4) from acute limb ischaemia (emergency, T5) is the central reasoning task; both must remain on the safety-net.

differential.diagnosis: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia of the right lower limb (Fontaine III / Rutherford 4), secondary to peripheral arterial disease
differential.evidence_basis: Ischaemic rest pain + absent distal pulses + elevation pallor/dependent rubor + anticipated ABI < 0.4 — the complete critical-limb-ischaemia picture; confirmation and revascularisation planning require in-person assessment, ABI and arterial imaging.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Definitive diagnosis and treatment (ABI, duplex/angiography, revascularisation) cannot be delivered by telehealth — the correct end state is urgent in-person vascular assessment, not a remote diagnosis.

red_flags.description: Ischaemic rest pain — severe distal foot pain at rest, worst lying flat and relieved by dependency
red_flags.clinical_significance: Rest pain marks the transition from claudication to critical limb ischaemia — the limb is now threatened.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Dismissing rest pain as worsening claudication delays urgent revascularisation and risks progression to tissue loss, gangrene and amputation.

red_flags.description: Absent distal pulses (dorsalis pedis, posterior tibial) with a diminished popliteal pulse on the right
red_flags.clinical_significance: Absent pedal pulses indicate critical macrovascular flow limitation and are a core objective sign of limb-threatening ischaemia.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Without recognising absent perfusion the severity is under-appreciated and referral is under-prioritised.

red_flags.description: Positive Buerger's test — elevation pallor with dependent rubor
red_flags.clinical_significance: Positional colour change reflects severely reduced arterial inflow that only just meets dependent resting demand.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing this sign underestimates how marginal perfusion is.

red_flags.description: Early atrophic skin changes on the great toe (pre-tissue-loss) — cool pale shiny skin with hair loss
red_flags.clinical_significance: Trophic changes signal chronic severe ischaemia at the threshold of ulceration and gangrene.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to act allows progression to frank ulceration and irreversible tissue loss.

red_flags.description: Potential progression to acute limb ischaemia — sudden cold, numb, paralysed or discoloured (white/blue/black) foot
red_flags.clinical_significance: Acute-on-chronic arterial occlusion threatens the limb within hours and is a surgical emergency (000).
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missing an acute deterioration or failing to safety-net for it can cost the limb within a single night.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Treating rest pain as a worse version of the year-long walking pain and under-triaging, rather than recognising the qualitative jump to a limb-threatening stage.
Reflexively working the painful leg up as a DVT; the cool, pale, pulseless limb is the opposite of the swollen, warm, erythematous venous picture.
Attributing the burning nocturnal foot pain to peripheral neuropathy and missing the vascular emergency.
Failing to screen for and safety-net the acute embolic/thrombotic occlusion mimic (sudden cold, numb, paralysed, pulseless limb) which needs 000, not an outpatient referral.
Advising leg elevation (correct for venous or musculoskeletal problems) — here it worsens ischaemic pain and perfusion; the limb must be kept dependent.
Attempting to diagnose or to prescribe strong (S8) analgesia remotely instead of arranging urgent in-person assessment where the limb can be examined and imaged.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Stage III (ischaemic rest pain, no tissue loss yet)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Chronic limb-threatening ischaemia — mandates urgent vascular referral for revascularisation assessment.
Limb viable but threatened; escalates toward category 5–6 (tissue loss/gangrene) without prompt intervention.

risk_scores.tool_name: Ankle-brachial index (anticipated, not measurable via telehealth)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: An ABI < 0.4 is consistent with critical ischaemia and a high risk of limb loss; requires formal in-person measurement.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Palpation of popliteal, dorsalis pedis and posterior tibial pulses
Buerger's test performed and graded by a clinician
Capillary refill time measured on examination
Skin temperature and true colour assessed on examination
Arterial duplex ultrasound and CT/MR angiography
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (emergency department / after-hours service) for limb-viability evaluation, analgesia and an urgent vascular surgery referral; instruct the patient to call 000 immediately if the foot becomes suddenly cold, numb, unable to move, or turns white/blue/black.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The diagnosis itself is fairly clear from history, but the real risks are UNDER-action (mislabelling as claudication/DVT/neuropathy and deferring to a routine GP appointment) and OVER-reach (attempting to diagnose definitively or prescribe opioids remotely instead of arranging urgent in-person care).

guidelines_referenced.reference: ESVS/SVS/WFVS Global Vascular Guidelines on Chronic Limb-Threatening Ischaemia (2019)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Suspected chronic limb-threatening ischaemia (ischaemic rest pain / tissue loss) warrants urgent referral to a vascular service for revascularisation assessment; antiplatelet and high-intensity statin therapy and immediate smoking cessation are cornerstones of medical management.

guidelines_referenced.reference: TASC II Inter-Society Consensus for the Management of Peripheral Arterial Disease
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Critical limb ischaemia is limb-threatening; best medical therapy (antiplatelet, statin, risk-factor modification) plus timely revascularisation is required to prevent amputation.
